insight: Lucina without Centurion loses to an opponent's Centurion, but Lucina combined with Centurion beats a lone Centurion. Going for Lucina too early without Centurion backup is a losing play when the opponent can respond with Centurion.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion about optimal sequencing of Centurion, Lucina, and related units in a set where all three were available.

insight: When both Centurion and Lucina are available, the ideal line is to acquire Centurion with a Synthesizer and Animus, then Lucina immediately afterward with a Fuge (Fuguesaw), getting all three units together rather than committing to one early.
author: p0lari
context: Expert discussion on the optimal build order when Centurion, Lucina, and related tech units coexist in a set.

insight: Iso Kronus and Gauss Cannon are very similar in nature; one of Iso Kronus's common uses is as extra Gauss Cannon supply in breachproof strategies. However, Gauss Cannon has higher 'inflation' (efficiency over time) because Iso Kronus misses a turn of attack due to its build time, making Gauss Cannon more efficient when its damage next turn will not simply be absorbed.
author: namington
context: Thread comparing the efficiency and use cases of Iso Kronus versus Gauss Cannon, including breachproof and burst contexts.

insight: Blood Pact is not viable as a long-term primary defense. It buys time to purchase more Blood Pacts, but if you are chaining them you generally need to land a disabling counter-attack within 4 turns, because by the 4th turn the Grimbotches generated will have negated a Pact's worth of defense each.
author: .bky_1556
context: Strategic analysis of Blood Pact's role in defense, specifically the Grimbotch drain degrading its defensive value over time.

insight: Blood Pact is strongest against one-time burst where the opponent won't break your absorb barrier after failing to breach, against other burst where Grimbotch damage can be soaked more efficiently than the last damage in the burst, as a stopgap when buying a large unit like Amporilla, and in high-inflation situations where 4 turns from now barely matters.
author: .bky_1556
context: Detailed community discussion enumerating specific scenarios where Blood Pact provides genuine value versus where it fails.

insight: Synchronizing two Scorchilla so they both fire on the same turn (ScSc/TT pattern) is generally slightly better than alternating (ScT/ScT), but the advantage is set-dependent and it is not worth going significantly out of your way to achieve synchronization.
author: masn6811
context: Community debate on whether Scorchilla should be synchronized or staggered, with masn6811 and bky both weighing in.

insight: A ScSc/TT/EvEv (double Scorchilla, double Tarsier, double Evolver) burst pattern is particularly powerful because it forces the opponent to go from needing no defense to needing both absorb and significant soak within a short window.
author: .bky_1556
context: Discussion of burst sequencing with Scorchilla and Tarsier combinations to maximize defensive pressure on the opponent.

insight: The term 'shock' as used by masn6811 refers specifically to units that provide more attack (plus chill) threat immediately than the red/blue/green tech resources spent on them, explicitly excluding build-time units. Units like Scorch and Bite formerly had shock but were nerfed to remove it; Grenade Mech is cited as a current example of a unit with shock.
author: masn6811
context: masn6811 explaining their personal metric 'shock' during a discussion about burst damage and unit design, with bky and darkeisbein probing the definition.

insight: Chieftain is most efficiently used by either sacrificing it at lifespan 3 in front of a Wall (soak) or sacrificing it at lifespan 1 in front of a Wall; absorbing with Chieftain is generally suboptimal except in high-inflation situations or when you need to time Lucina value on its final turn. It is almost never correct to sacrifice a Wall in front of a lifespan-3 Chieftain.
author: consensus
context: Extended expert discussion comparing Chieftain usage patterns with Deleted User and masn6811 converging on optimal sacrifice vs. absorb decisions.

insight: In sets with Grimbotch and Feral Warden, the correct opening is a fast red start using Conduit to build a Feral Warden each turn, then absorbing 2 on the Feral before leaving it as an attacker, transitioning into Grimbotches afterward.
author: darkeisbein
context: Advice given to a newer player who was overrun by a Tarsier/Grimbotch ramp in a set containing Rectifier, Grimbotch, and Feral Warden.

insight: Going second is generally advantageous in Prismata because the second player receives one extra Drone, except in 'big blue' sets where first player's tempo advantage with blue tech outweighs the extra Drone.
author: f300xen
context: Discussion about first-player vs. second-player advantages in the context of Zemora Voidbringer opening lines.

insight: Zemora Voidbringer's cost has changed multiple times through patches: masn6811 recalls it being 6GG and 5GG in older versions; at the time of this conversation (March 2018) it costs 5GGG.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion triggered by a wiki opening line for Zemora that players found inconsistent with the current cost.
